title: Lockheed Lands $35B THAAD Mega-Contract, NATO Preps Ankara Summit Billions
subhead: A $35B THAAD production contract, NATO's upcoming tens-of-billions arms announcement, FCAS fallout reshaping European combat air, and a G7 that shifted the terms on Ukraine air defense licenses — the defense industrial pipeline is widening on every front.

sections.heading: Lockheed Wins $35B THAAD Contract, Quadrupling Production
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin secured a over $35 billion, seven-year contract from the U.S. Missile Defense Agency to quadruple THAAD interceptor production — from 96 to 400 interceptors per year. The award, announced June 24, is one of the first major multiyear procurement contracts under the Pentagon's Acquisition Transformation Strategy, designed to accelerate defense industrial output at lower cost through fixed-price contract line items.

The contract runs from March 2026 through June 2032, with $842 million in FY2026 procurement funds obligated at award. Lockheed broke ground on a Munitions Production Center in Troy, Alabama, in March and a Munitions Acceleration Center in Camden, Arkansas, in January — part of a $9 million investment through 2030 in weapons manufacturing facilities.

A May CSIS analysis warned that restoring U.S. munitions stockpiles to pre-Iran war levels will take at least three years, creating a 'window of vulnerability' for a potential Western Pacific conflict. Lockheed has also signed agreements this year to boost PAC-3 MSE interceptor and Precision Strike Missile (PrSM) production.
sections.whyItMatters: The THAAD contract is the largest single missile defense award in recent memory and signals that the post-Iran war munitions replenishment drive is translating into concrete multiyear orders — not just pledges. The fixed-price structure and seven-year timeline give Lockheed visibility and scale, but the real test is whether the industrial base can staff and equip the new facilities fast enough to close the stockpile gap before the Pacific vulnerability window widens.

sections.heading: NATO Set to Announce Tens of Billions in New Defense Contracts at Ankara Summit
sections.body:
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said on June 25 that the alliance will announce 'tens of billions of dollars' in new defense contracts at the upcoming Ankara summit in July. Speaking in Washington, Rutte emphasized that 'we need to ensure that we are translating our economic might into military capabilities', adding that the summit will also reaffirm the alliance's commitment to defense spending goals and support for Ukraine.

The announcement follows NATO's recent agreement at The Hague to raise the defense spending target to 5% of GDP by 2035 — split between a 3.5% core military benchmark and 1.5% for strategic resilience, cybersecurity, and industrial readiness. The deal marks a dramatic shift from the previous 2% benchmark. Poland and the U.S. already exceed the new targets, but Spain, Italy, and Belgium have voiced concerns about feasibility — Spain's PM Pedro Sanchez declared 2% of GDP 'sufficient, realistic and compatible with the welfare state'.

Italy's defense minister Guido Crosetto called 5% 'unthinkable', while Belgium's foreign minister Maxime Prevot labeled it 'excessive'. University of Birmingham professor Stefan Wolff warned the target risks becoming 'financial inflation without strategic output' without prioritization and coordinated defense-industrial development.
sections.whyItMatters: Rutte's figure — 'tens of billions' — suggests the Ankara summit will be as much about procurement deals as political declarations. If the contracts materialize, they'll reshape the European defense industrial base. But the 5% target is already straining alliance cohesion: southern European states are openly balking, and the gap between pledges and procurement capacity remains wide.

sections.heading: FCAS Collapse Opens European Fighter Landscape to GCAP and National Programs
sections.body:
The Franco-German-Spanish Future Combat Air System (FCAS) was formally cancelled on June 8, ending nine years of trilateral work on a sixth-generation fighter. Chancellor Friedrich Merz and President Emmanuel Macron agreed to scrap the Next Generation Fighter element after industrial mediation between Dassault Aviation and Airbus Defence and Space failed to resolve disputes over leadership, workshare, and intellectual property.

Spain, as junior partner, aligned largely with German positions but expressed disappointment. Some FCAS sub-elements — drone initiatives and combat cloud technologies — may continue as standalone projects, but the manned fighter is gone.

The UK's rival Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), partnering Italy and Japan, immediately moved to position itself as the alternative. The UK Ministry of Defence reiterated that GCAP 'has been designed with allies and partners at its heart' and remains open to additional nations — as long as 'programme delivery is assured.' BAE Systems leads the UK effort alongside Leonardo (Italy) and Japan Aircraft Industrial Enhancement (Japan), targeting a 2035 in-service date.

India, which had been considering FCAS as an option for its own sixth-gen fighter requirements, now faces a narrower choice. French far-right leader Jordan Bardella claimed vindication, arguing that France should have pursued a national program from the start.
sections.whyItMatters: FCAS is the biggest European defense program collapse in a generation. The question now is whether Dassault goes it alone on a next-gen fighter, whether Germany and Spain join GCAP, or whether the fragmentation creates openings for U.S. platforms. The 2035 GCAP timeline means Europe will rely on current-gen fighters for another decade minimum — a gap Russia and China won't ignore.

sections.heading: G7 Breaks Taboo on Ukraine Air Defense Production Licenses
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, President Volodymyr Zelensky secured a shift in Western posture on air defense production. G7 leaders issued a joint statement saying they are 'ready to consider extending to Ukraine the benefit of licenses to allow for an increase in Ukraine's military production' — and for the first time, according to Zelensky, 'the American team responded positively to the licenses'.

Trump plans to ask American defense companies to manufacture missiles for air defense systems under license in Ukraine and Europe, Zelensky said. The focus is on Patriot interceptor production — Ukraine says it has the technical capability to start producing Patriot missiles domestically but needs U.S. licensing approval.

The shift comes amid a worsening air defense shortage. Zelensky sent Trump a letter in late May warning of 'critical missile defense shortages', particularly anti-ballistic capabilities, writing: 'When it comes to defending against ballistic missiles, we rely almost exclusively on the United States.'

Ukraine's recent strikes on Moscow — including a major drone attack on the Kapotnya oil refinery on June 18 that Russian authorities called the largest attack on the capital since the war began — demonstrated offensive reach, but the air defense gap remains acute. Russia struck Kyiv's Pechersk Lavra monastery on June 15, killing civilians and damaging the 11th-century religious site.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time the U.S. has signaled willingness to let Ukraine produce Western-design air defense missiles under license — a step beyond merely shipping systems. If it happens, it would shorten Patriot supply chains dramatically and build long-term Ukrainian industrial capacity. But it also transfers sensitive missile tech to an active war zone, with all the escalation and proliferation risks that entails.

sections.heading: Belarus Builds Border War Infrastructure; Crimea Fuel Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
Ukrainian intelligence has identified five border areas where Belarus is constructing road infrastructure with ammunition and fuel storage bases directly along Ukraine's border — Kobrin-Kovel, Ivanovo-Manevychi, Lunynets-Sarny, Rechytsa-Korosten, and Homel-Cherniviv. Zelensky described the construction as proceeding under 'obvious Russian influence', with Russian documents referencing the infrastructure in the context of Moscow's invasion tasks.

Belarus switched off retransmitters used to guide Russian Shahed drones toward Ukraine, according to briefings from Commander-in-Chief Syrsky and Ukraine's intelligence service — a partial de-escalation after Zelensky issued a one-week ultimatum to Lukashenko on June 19. But Belarus's opposition United Transitional Cabinet submitted a 30-page report to Ukraine's Foreign Ministry warning that Lukashenko may be preparing for war, citing constitutional changes removing Belarus's neutral status, a fivefold increase in defense spending, and a 50% growth in contract soldiers.

In Russian-occupied Crimea, occupation authorities suspended all civilian fuel sales on June 21, restricting gasoline and diesel to emergency and state vehicles, after Ukrainian drone strikes hit fuel terminals in Kerch and at Port Kavkaz near the Kerch Strait. Ukrainian intelligence reports the fuel, logistics, and governance crisis in Crimea is 'daily deepening' — with Russia's occupation administration acknowledging its inability to resolve the problems.
sections.whyItMatters: Belarus remains the most dangerous unknown on Ukraine's northern flank — oscillating between concessions (switching off Shahed guidance) and escalatory preparations (border infrastructure, defense spending surge). Meanwhile, Ukraine's systematic campaign against Crimean logistics is producing measurable results: civilian fuel rationing signals real strain on Russia's supply lines to the occupied peninsula.

sections.heading: US Builds War-Ready Stockpile in Australia; France-UK Deploy for Hormuz; Russian Warship Fires on UK Yacht
sections.body:
The U.S. Marine Corps is building its first permanent weapons stockpile in Australia, with $30 million allocated for warehouses and offices in Victoria, on Australia's southeast coast — beyond the range of most Chinese missiles. The tender documents, first reported by SCMP, signal a shift from rotational to enduring logistics posture. Australia's defense minister described an expanding U.S. military footprint in the country.

In the Middle East, a joint France-UK naval mission is readying to deploy to the Strait of Hormuz following a U.S.-Iran interim peace deal. The Charles de Gaulle carrier group transited the Suez Canal with 20 Rafale jets, while HMS Dragon is already operating alongside French escort vessels. France24 reports the memorandum of understanding between Paris and London was signed in May, with the mission designed to help reopen and secure shipping through the chokepoint.

Closer to home, a Russian warship — the guided-missile frigate Admiral Grigorovich — fired warning shots at a UK-registered yacht, Bright Future, in the English Channel. The Russian Defence Ministry claimed the yacht made a 'dangerous approach'; the British couple on board said they 'did nothing wrong.' UK officials called the incident a reminder that 'Russia poses a direct threat to our nation'.
sections.whyItMatters: The Australia stockpile is a quiet but significant Indo-Pacific development — permanent pre-positioned war stocks shorten the logistics tail for any Taiwan contingency. The Hormuz mission shows European navies stepping into gaps the U.S. is pulling back from. And the Channel incident is a reminder that Russian naval assertiveness extends well beyond the Black Sea.

sections.heading: Aselsan Inks $905M Air Defense Contract for Turkey's Steel Dome; Siper 2 Passes Test
sections.body:
Turkish defense giant Aselsan signed a €780 million ($905 million) contract with the Presidency of Defense Industries (SSB) for air defense systems aimed at strengthening the country's Steel Dome multilayered air defense architecture. Steel Dome is designed as a domestic 'system-of-systems' covering all altitudes and ranges — from low-altitude counter-drone layers (Korkut, Gurz) through the Hisar mid-tier systems to the Siper long-range upper layer.

The contract also covers the Hakim command-and-control system, which coordinates all Steel Dome components with sensor fusion and AI-driven threat assessment.

Separately, the SSB announced that Siper 2, the next iteration of Turkey's long-range air and missile defense system, successfully completed a firing test against a maneuvering target. SSB head Haluk Görgün said Siper 2 'will assume duties at longer ranges and higher altitudes compared to Siper 1.'

Turkey has also signed $6.5 billion in contracts to reinforce Steel Dome, according to a November 2025 Reuters report.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is building an indigenous integrated air defense ecosystem that could reduce its dependence on Russian S-400s and Western systems — and potentially become an export competitor in the Middle Eastern and Asian markets. The Siper 2 test is particularly significant as NATO's Ankara summit approaches, positioning Turkey as both host and a rising defense industrial power.

bottomLine: The defense industrial pipeline is widening rapidly on every front: a $35B THAAD contract signals the post-Iran war munitions replenishment is underway, NATO is preparing to announce tens of billions in new arms deals at Ankara, and the G7 just broke the taboo on letting Ukraine produce Patriot missiles under license. Meanwhile, the FCAS collapse has fragmented Europe's fighter jet future, Turkey is scaling its Steel Dome into a credible integrated air defense system, and the U.S. is quietly pre-positioning war stocks in Australia. The common thread: governments are moving from spending pledges to concrete procurement at a pace that will stress industrial capacity across every sector.
sources: Defense News, Lockheed Martin, CSIS, Politico, Anadolu Agency, Reuters, AA, TRT World, UK Defence Journal, Kyiv Independent, Kyiv Post, Daily Sabah, Naval News, SCMP, Channel News Asia, France24, Defense Talks, AP, BBC, Georgia Today, China Daily, LBC, AFP, MSN
